content:
3 min read
Just now

--

The world is presently in one of the largest transitions on all levels of society that has happened in the last 100 years. Transition is an ongoing phenomenon. Today we have political, social, economic, philosophical and technological change that will bring to the new generation a world view that the past generations do not comprehend and really desire. The problem is that the individual today has little or no say into the direction society is taking, as it has come under the control of technology (internet, social media and AI) and large multinational corporations that have as their sole concern profit. The democracy in the west has turned to a voyeuristic capitalistic democracy that has left culture, humanity and community behind. In parallel, the technologies of the world have taken over the thinking and creativity of the mass of people who are seemingly bowing to the algorithms that technology is creating. In a manner one can question the very need of the human being in the future society, as the human race is handing its minds to technology to do everything for mankind. Technologies have their place without a doubt, but the question to be answered is will technology control the human race, or will the human race control technology?
What is being forgotten with the rise of fundamentalism on all levels, is what will happen to culture? Cultural heritage has been the glue that has kept societies together for centuries and created the basis of democratic thought, free thought and a functional society. Even society at the best of times has never been an art of perfection, yet it is the basis of collective consciousness, identity and community. Will a society without this be a ship lost at sea, with no rudder to guide it through the turbulence? Maybe it is time for community and society to create a new framework that will allow the transfer of the knowledge, art, architecture, city, literature and monuments of civilized history before it gets lost to the algorithm.
Will the new generation through technology lose touch with the past, as their main concern is today, and what social media presents in 2-to-10-minute videos as being important. This has created two outer worlds, the real outer world and the pseudo-outer world (via algorithm), and in turn created in most humans two Selves: the real Self and the pseudo-Self of social media. Yet with these two outer world formulations, where will cultural heritage stand relative to daily life? Will the next generation slowly turn away from the technological world and understand the need for communication, dialogue and cultural knowledge?
It is now the time for humanity to create a framework protecting the cultural legacy that is civilized history. Presently this is being protected by the few in art history, architecture, art and literature. These pockets of intelligentsia must be expanded through communication with the future generations. If there is no masterplan to cultural heritage, mankind may be taught that culture started with social media during a time of voyeuristic capitalistic democracy, where the destruction of culture is expanding. What will be next, the tearing down of the Colosseum in Rome for a series of new high rises? The choice is ours, but are we intelligent enough today to comprehend this?
